title: Angled-Leg Sawhorses for Desks
summary: A pair of splayed-leg 2x2 sawhorses with a built-in shelf between the legs — sturdy enough to carry a desktop, useful enough to keep around after.
description:
"Adjustable" in the source name refers to build height, not an in-use adjustment — there's no hardware here for changing height after assembly. What you actually get is a pair of matched sawhorses with their legs splayed at 15° for real stability under a desk-width span, plus a shelf built into the lower half of each leg assembly that most sawhorse plans skip entirely.

Build both sawhorses' leg assemblies side by side and compare them before final assembly — a pair of desk sawhorses only works as a matched set, and any height or angle mismatch between the two is the kind of thing you don't notice until a desktop is rocking on top of them.

tools.note: Recommended for the leg joints; nails and glue also work.

cutList.note: Both ends cut parallel at 15° off square.
Both ends cut not parallel at 15° off square, longest point measurement.
Both ends cut not parallel at 15°, longest point measurement.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut eight leg pieces at 25-7/8", four top pieces at 12-3/4" and four cross braces at 12" from the Pine 2x2. Cut four top gussets at 5-7/8", four inside blocks at 3", four horizontal supports at 18-1/2", eight shelf boards at 21-1/2" and four end caps at 23" from the Pine 1x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to four 8 ft Pine 2x2 and four 8 ft Pine 1x3. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the leg pairs
steps.body: Build two matching legs for each sawhorse you're making. Pocket holes work well for attaching the top piece to each leg pair, but nailing and gluing it on works too.

steps.title: Attach the inside blocks
steps.body: Nail and glue the inside blocking piece onto each leg assembly.

steps.title: Attach the horizontal supports
steps.body: Nail and glue the horizontal support pieces to the two leg assemblies, joining them into the finished sawhorse shape. Compare both sawhorses side by side here — this is the point to catch any height or angle mismatch before it's locked in.

steps.title: Add the shelf boards
steps.body: Nail and glue the shelf boards to the bottom horizontal supports, spaced evenly across the span between the legs.

images.alt: A pair of matched wood sawhorses with splayed legs and a built-in lower shelf
